era_context: The Ur III dynasty in Mesopotamia is in its final decades under Ibbi-Sin, facing severe external pressure. Egypt is fragmented in the First Intermediate Period. The Indus Valley Civilization is at its mature phase. Traditional Chinese historiography places the Xia dynasty in its early-to-middle period. The Bronze Age is well-established across Eurasia.

events.title: Ibbi-Sin's reign: Ur III state in terminal decline
events.description: Ibbi-Sin (r. c. 2028–2004 BCE) continues to rule the Ur III state, but the dynasty is in its final years. Elamite pressure from the east and Amorite infiltration from the west have severely weakened central authority. Cuneiform administrative texts show continued fragmentation of provincial control and economic strain. The unified Sumerian state that had dominated Mesopotamia for nearly a century is collapsing. Within a few years, Ibbi-Sin will be captured by the Elamites, ending the Ur III dynasty.

sources.name: Ur III cuneiform administrative texts and king lists

events.certainty_note: Ibbi-Sin's reign is well-attested in cuneiform sources. Absolute dating to -2009 relies on conventional Mesopotamian chronology (±50 years). The year falls within his reign with high probability.

events.title: Harappan civilization: mature phase at its height
events.region: Indus Valley (modern Pakistan and northwest India)
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization is at or near its maximum extent and complexity. Major urban centers including Mohenjo-daro, Harappa, Dholavira, and Lothal are thriving. Standardized weights, measures, and seals indicate sophisticated economic organization. Long-distance trade with Mesopotamia, the Persian Gulf, and Central Asia is well-established. The undeciphered Indus script appears on seals and pottery. The civilization shows no signs of major disruption at this time.
events.certainty_note: The Harappan mature phase is well-established archaeologically. The period c. 2600–1900 BCE is well-dated; -2009 falls within this period with high confidence.

disconfirming_evidence: No significant disconfirming evidence identified for events listed.
historiographic_note: Documentation for -2009 is sparse and regionally uneven. Mesopotamian cuneiform sources are abundant but administrative in nature. The Indus Valley is known only through archaeology and undeciphered inscriptions. Egypt is known through later sources and archaeology. China is known only through traditional historiography. Large regions of the world are absent from the historical record.

graph_edges.note: Ibbi-Sin's reign leads to the fall of Ur III
